# Phase 1 — Base Camp (Weeks 1-3)

> **Phase:** Accumulation / Base Building | **Duration:** March 16 - April 5, 2026
> **Weight Target:** 89 kg → 85 kg (gradual deficit ~1.3 kg/week)
> **Training Focus:** Movement quality, baseline establishment, progressive overload
> **RPE Progression:** Week 1: 6-7 | Week 2: 7-8 | Week 3: 8-9
> **1RMs:** Squat 150 kg | Deadlift 180 kg | Bench 115 kg | OHP 75 kg

---

## Phase 1 Overview

Phase 1 builds the **aerobic and structural foundation** the entire camp depends on. The goal is not to destroy yourself in Week 1 — it's to establish patterns, dial in technique under moderate load, and begin a sustainable caloric deficit that will carry through all 9 weeks.

Strength work uses **linear progressive overload** across the 3 weeks. Loads start at 65-70% 1RM and climb to 80-85% by Week 3. MMA sessions focus on volume and technique accumulation — sparring is limited to once per week (Thursday) and starts at controlled intensity.

### Phase 1 Goals

- Establish 2x/day training rhythm (AM strength + PM MMA)
- Build aerobic base with 2-3 cardiac output sessions per week
- Progressive overload on all compound lifts
- One hard sparring session per week (Thursday)
- Begin caloric deficit (~500 kcal/day → ~0.5 kg/week from diet alone)
- Baseline conditioning test (Week 0 or early Week 1)
- Establish morning weight tracking habit

### Tempo Key

All strength movements use the following tempo notation unless otherwise noted:
- **3** = 3 sec eccentric (lowering)
- **1** = 1 sec pause at bottom
- **2** = 2 sec concentric (controlled lift)
- **0** = no pause at top

Week 3 shifts to **3-1-X-0** on compound lifts (X = explosive concentric) to prepare for Phase 2.

### Intensity Key

| Marker | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 🟢 | Easy — recovery pace, low CNS demand |
| 🟡 | Moderate — working hard but controlled |
| 🔴 | Hard — high intensity, high CNS demand |

## Monday — Week 1

**Warm-Up** (10 min) — Protocol 1: Upper Body Warm-Up (see Training Tools).

| Exercise | Sets x Reps | Load | Rest | Tempo | RPE | Notes |
|----------|-------------|------|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Bench Press | 4 x 6 | 75 kg (65%) | 2.5 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Control the eccentric. Full ROM, scapulae retracted. |
| Barbell Row | 4 x 6 | 70 kg | 2.5 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Pull to lower sternum. Torso stays still. No heaving. |
| Overhead Press | 3 x 8 | 45 kg (60%) | 2 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Strict press. Brace core, press straight up. |
| Weighted Pull-Up | 3 x 6 | BW + 10 kg | 2 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Dead hang start. Full extension at bottom. |
| Incline DB Press | 3 x 10 | 28 kg each | 90 s | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | 30-degree incline. Controlled path. |
| Face Pulls | 3 x 15 | Light cable | 60 s | 2-1-1-0 | 5 | Prehab. External rotation at top. |
| Pallof Press | 3 x 10/side | Band/cable | 60 s | 2-1-2-0 | 6 | Anti-rotation. Full arm extension. |

**Session Total:** 23 working sets, ~50 min

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Pec doorway stretch 2x30s/side, banded lat stretch 2x30s/side, thoracic foam roll 60s.

# Phase 2 — Intensification (Weeks 4-6)

> **Phase:** Transmutation / Peak Volume | **Duration:** April 6 - April 26, 2026
> **Weight Target:** 85 kg → 81 kg (continue ~1.3 kg/week deficit)
> **Training Focus:** Peak training volume, hardest conditioning, increased sparring
> **RPE Progression:** Week 4: 7-8 | Week 5: 8-9 | Week 6: 8-9 (peak)
> **1RMs:** Squat 150 kg | Deadlift 180 kg | Bench 115 kg | OHP 75 kg

---

## Phase 2 Overview

This is the **hardest phase of camp**. Everything escalates: sparring frequency doubles to twice per week, conditioning sessions layer on top of each other, and fight simulation circuits appear regularly. Strength work shifts from progression to **maintenance** — loads stay at 80-85% 1RM with reduced volume so that recovery resources can be directed toward MMA-specific work and sparring.

### Key Changes from Phase 1

| Element | Phase 1 | Phase 2 |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Sparring | 1x/week (Thu) | **2x/week (Tue PM + Thu PM)** |
| Strength Goal | Progressive overload | **Maintenance (80-85% 1RM)** |
| Conditioning | Single session days | **Double conditioning on some days** |
| Tempo | 3-1-1-0 (varied) | **3-1-X-0 (explosive concentric throughout)** |
| Rest Periods | Standard (2-3 min) | **Tightened on conditioning work** |
| Accessories | General | **MMA-specific replacements** |
| Fight Simulation | Occasional | **Frequent (2-3x/week)** |
| Opponent Drilling | None | **Integrated into technique sessions** |

### Tempo Key

All strength movements use **3-1-X-0** unless otherwise noted:
- **3** = 3 sec eccentric (lowering)
- **1** = 1 sec pause at bottom
- **X** = eXplosive concentric (as fast as possible)
- **0** = no pause at top

### Intensity Key

| Marker | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 🟢 | Easy — recovery pace, low CNS demand |
| 🟡 | Moderate — working hard but controlled |
| 🔴 | Hard — high intensity, high CNS demand, full effort |

## Mid-Phase Checkpoint — After Week 5

Complete the following assessments on Sunday evening or Monday morning before Week 6 begins.

### Body Composition Check

| Metric | Target | Action if Off-Track |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| Weight | ~81.5 kg | If above 82.5 kg: tighten nutrition, add 10 min Zone 2 to Tuesday/Saturday AM. If below 81 kg: increase carbs slightly — do not undercut performance. |
| Visual | Noticeably leaner than Phase 1 start | If holding water: check sodium, sleep quality, and stress. |

### Performance Markers

| Test | Minimum Standard | Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------|
| Resting HR | Stable or decreasing from Phase 1 | If elevated >5 BPM: overreaching — reduce Week 6 volume 10%. |
| HRV | Stable or within normal range | Significant drop = overreaching signal. |
| Grip Strength | No decline from Phase 1 baseline | Drop = CNS fatigue indicator. |
| Mood / Motivation | Manageable | Some fatigue is normal. If motivation is critically low, reduce Week 6 sparring to 1x. |
| Sleep Quality | 7+ hours average | If poor: prioritize sleep hygiene over extra training. |
| Injury Check | No new injuries | Any joint pain, persistent soreness, or swelling = modify. Do not push through injuries this close to fight. |

### Decision Point

| Condition | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| All markers green | Proceed with full Week 6 programming |
| Resting HR elevated or HRV suppressed | Reduce Week 6 conditioning volume by 15-20%. Maintain sparring. |
| Joint pain or injury | Modify around the issue. If serious, convert Week 6 to modified taper. Consult medical. |
| Weight ahead of schedule (<81 kg) | Add carbs to performance meals. Prioritize recovery. |
| Weight behind schedule (>83 kg) | Add 10 min Zone 2 daily. Tighten nutrition. DO NOT crash diet. |
| Mental burnout | Reduce volume 10%. Add fun training — open mat, flow rolling, playful sparring. |

## Phase 3 Overview

This phase is about **subtraction, not addition**. You are not building fitness here — you are sharpening what you already have and allowing your body to supercompensate. Every session has a purpose. If it doesn't make you sharper for fight night, cut it.

### Key Principles

- **Strength drops to 2 sessions/week** (Monday and Wednesday only — Friday power session is removed)
- **Loads drop to 70-75% of 1RM** with emphasis on bar speed and explosiveness
- **No hard sparring in Week 8** — light technical sparring only
- **Week 7** — moderate sparring is still permitted (controlled rounds, trusted partners only)
- **Conditioning becomes shorter and sharper** — intervals under 10 seconds, full recovery between sets
- **Game plan specific drilling increases** — positional sparring, situational reps, entries and exits
- **Mental preparation begins** — daily visualization, fight strategy review, routine-setting
- **Water loading protocol discussion** — coordinate with coach and reference weight-management section

# Phase 4 — Fight Week (Week 9)

> **Phase:** Fight Week | **Duration:** May 11 - May 16, 2026
> **Weight:** 79 kg → 77 kg (water cut)
> **Focus:** Light technical work, weight management, mental preparation
> **Training:** Minimal — shadow boxing, visualization, light movement only

---

## Fight Week Overview

This is not a training week. This is a **preparation and execution** week. The work is done. Your only jobs now are:

1. Make weight safely
2. Stay mentally sharp
3. Arrive at the fight healthy, hydrated, and confident

The 2 kg water cut from 79 kg to 77 kg is a **mild cut** and should be manageable with proper water loading and sodium manipulation. Follow the protocols below exactly as planned with your coach.

---

## Water Loading Protocol

Begin water loading on Sunday. The goal is to upregulate your body's water excretion mechanisms by drinking large volumes, then sharply reducing intake so the body continues flushing water even when intake drops.

| Day | Date | Water Intake | Notes |
|-----|------|-------------|-------|
| Sunday | May 11 | 8 L | Begin water loading. Spread across waking hours. |
| Monday | May 12 | 8 L | Continue loading. Urine should be clear and frequent. |
| Tuesday | May 13 | 6 L | Begin tapering water. Body still excreting at high rate. |
| Wednesday | May 14 | 4 L | Further reduction. Noticeable water loss begins. |
| Thursday | May 15 | 2 L | Minimal water. Sip throughout day. Stop by evening. |
| Friday | May 16 | Sips only | Small sips until weigh-in. Full rehydration after. |

**Key Rules:**
- Spread water intake evenly across the day (do not chug large volumes at once)
- Distilled water is preferred during loading days (lower mineral content)
- Track all fluid intake — use a measured bottle
- Weigh yourself morning and evening to track progress
- If weight is not trending on schedule, consult coach before adjusting

---

## Sodium Manipulation Protocol

Sodium manipulation works alongside water loading. High sodium early causes water retention; dropping sodium causes the body to flush stored water.

| Day | Date | Sodium Intake | Food Notes |
|-----|------|--------------|------------|
| Sunday | May 11 | Normal (~3,000-4,000 mg) | Eat normally, add salt to food |
| Monday | May 12 | Normal (~3,000-4,000 mg) | Continue normal salting |
| Tuesday | May 13 | Reduced (~1,500-2,000 mg) | Begin reducing — avoid processed food, no added salt |
| Wednesday | May 14 | Low (~500-1,000 mg) | Plain foods only — rice, chicken, vegetables, no seasoning |
| Thursday | May 15 | Minimal (~200-500 mg) | Very plain food, small portions, no added salt |
| Friday | May 16 | None until post-weigh-in | No food until after weigh-in |

**Carbohydrate Notes:**
- Sunday-Tuesday: Normal carb intake
- Wednesday: Reduce carbs to ~100g (reduces glycogen-bound water)
- Thursday: Reduce carbs to ~50g
- Friday: No food until post-weigh-in

## Rehydration Protocol (Post-Weigh-In)

The goal is to rehydrate from ~77 kg back toward ~79-80 kg before the fight. You have approximately 8-10 hours to rehydrate.

| Timeframe | Fluid | Food | Target |
|-----------|-------|------|--------|
| 0-30 min post-weigh-in | 500 mL ORS (oral rehydration solution) | Banana, white bread + honey | Begin absorption |
| 30-90 min | 500 mL ORS, sip steadily | Pre-fight Meal 1 (see below) | |
| 90 min - 3 hours | 500 mL water/electrolyte | Pre-fight Meal 2 (see below) | ~1.5 L consumed |
| 3-6 hours | Sip water/electrolytes as desired | Light snacks as desired | ~2-2.5 L consumed |
| 6-8 hours | Small sips only | Final small snack (2 hrs before fight) | ~3 L total consumed |

**Rehydration Rules:**
- **NEVER chug water.** Sip steadily. Chugging causes bloating, nausea, and poor absorption.
- Use an oral rehydration solution (ORS) or add electrolyte tabs to water (Pedialyte, LMNT, or similar)
- Do NOT drink plain water only — you need sodium and potassium to retain fluid
- Stop drinking large volumes 2 hours before fight time
- Target total fluid intake: 2.5-3.5 L between weigh-in and fight
- Monitor urine color — aim for pale yellow, not clear (clear means you're flushing, not retaining)

## Pre-Fight Meal Plan

All food should be **easily digestible, familiar, and not gas-producing.** Do not eat anything new on fight day.

| White rice | 1.5 cups cooked | Easy carbs, familiar |

| Food | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| White rice or white pasta | 1 cup cooked | Carb loading |
| Lean protein (chicken/fish) | 100g | |
| White bread | 1-2 slices | Easily digestible carbs |
| Small amount of fruit | Banana or melon | |

### Final Pre-Fight Snack (~2 hours before fight)

| Food | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Rice cake | 2 | Light, easy carbs |
| Honey | 1 tbsp | Quick energy |
| Banana | Half | |
| Sips of electrolyte drink | 100-200 mL | |

**Foods to AVOID on fight day:**
- Dairy (bloating, gas)
- High fiber foods (beans, whole grains, raw vegetables)
- Spicy food
- Fatty or fried food
- Anything new or unusual
- Large volumes of any single food
- Caffeine in excess (one small coffee is fine if habitual)

## Warning Signs During the Water Cut

Monitor for these warning signs throughout the week. **If any STOP signs appear, halt the cut immediately and seek medical advice.**

### Normal / Expected
| Symptom | Notes |
|---------|-------|
| Mild thirst | Expected from Wednesday onward |
| Slightly reduced energy | Normal, conserve energy |
| Darker urine | Expected as water intake drops |
| Mild irritability | Normal — manage with breathing exercises |
| Slight headache (brief) | Can occur, usually resolves |
| Dry mouth | Expected Thursday/Friday |

### Caution — Slow Down
| Symptom | Action |
|---------|--------|
| Persistent headache | Small sips of electrolyte solution, reassess in 30 min |
| Mild dizziness when standing | Sit down, sip electrolytes, move slowly |
| Muscle cramps | Electrolytes needed — small sips of ORS |
| Extreme irritability / mood swings | Rest, breathe, reduce any remaining activity |
| Poor sleep quality | Accept it, stay in bed, rest even if not sleeping |

### STOP — Seek Help Immediately
| Symptom | Action |
|---------|--------|
| Severe dizziness or fainting | **STOP CUT.** Drink electrolytes. Call coach/doctor. |
| Rapid or irregular heartbeat | **STOP CUT.** Seek medical attention immediately. |
| Confusion or disorientation | **STOP CUT.** This is dangerous. Rehydrate now. |
| Inability to stand | **STOP CUT.** Medical emergency possible. |
| Chest pain | **STOP CUT. CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES.** |
| Vomiting (unable to keep fluids down) | **STOP CUT.** Seek medical help — dehydration will worsen. |
| Seizures or muscle spasms (severe) | **STOP CUT. CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES.** |

> **Remember:** No fight is worth your health. A 2 kg water cut is mild and should be straightforward. If it becomes anything more than mildly uncomfortable, something is wrong. Speak up.

# Daily Nutrition & Meal Plan — Weight Cut Camp

**Fighter Profile:** 89 kg → 77 kg | **Fight Date:** May 16, 2026 | **Camp:** 9 Weeks (March 16 – May 16)

> This plan provides specific daily meal options for each phase of the weight cut. Every meal slot has 2–3 options for variety. Protein is kept at 2.3 g/kg throughout to preserve muscle during the caloric deficit. Carbohydrates are cycled by training demand. Fat is kept at the minimum needed for hormonal health.

---

## Quick Reference — Phase Calorie & Macro Targets

| Phase | Weeks | Body Weight (approx.) | Training Day | Rest Day | Protein | Notes |
|-------|-------|-----------------------|-------------|----------|---------|-------|
| **Phase 1** | 1–3 | 89→85 kg | ~2,600 kcal | ~2,100 kcal | ~200 g (2.3 g/kg × 87) | Base cut, establish deficit |
| **Phase 2** | 4–6 | 85→81 kg | ~2,400 kcal | ~1,900 kcal | ~191 g (2.3 g/kg × 83) | Deeper deficit |
| **Phase 3** | 7–8 | 81→79 kg | ~2,400 kcal | ~2,000 kcal | ~184 g (2.3 g/kg × 80) | Protect performance |
| **Phase 4** | 9 | 79→77 kg | Minimal/strategic | — | Reduced | Water/sodium cut |

---

## General Guidelines

- **Meal prep:** Cook protein in bulk (chicken breast, lean beef, eggs) on Sundays and Wednesdays.
- **Weigh food raw** (meat, rice, oats) for accuracy.
- **Hydrate:** Water intake targets are listed per phase. Drink consistently throughout the day — do not chug before meals.
- **Supplements (all phases):** Multivitamin, 5 g creatine (drop in Phase 4), fish oil 2 g, magnesium 400 mg before bed.
- **Sodium:** Keep moderate in Phases 1–3 (~2,000–2,500 mg/day). Manipulated in Phase 4.
- **Caffeine:** Black coffee or green tea only — no sugar, no milk during cut (milk adds unnecessary calories).

**Electrolytes:** Continue electrolyte tabs during training. Maintain sodium at ~2,500 mg/day until water loading begins.

> **Phase 3 notes:** Deficit is slightly reduced to protect fight performance. Food selection stays clean and simple. Begin water loading at the END of Week 8 (drink 6–8 L/day for 3 days leading into fight week). This sets up the water cut.

---

## Phase 3 — TRAINING DAY (~2,400 kcal)

---

## Phase 3 — Water Loading Protocol (End of Week 8)

Begin 3 days before fight week (Thursday–Saturday of Week 8):

| Day | Water Intake | Sodium | Notes |
|-----|-------------|--------|-------|
| **Thursday** | 7 L | 3,000 mg (add salt to meals) | Begin overloading |
| **Friday** | 8 L | 3,500 mg | Peak loading |
| **Saturday** | 8 L | 3,500 mg | Maintain — body adjusts to high output |

> This conditions the body to excrete large volumes of water. When water intake drops dramatically in fight week, the body continues excreting at the elevated rate, resulting in rapid weight loss.

---

# PHASE 4 — FIGHT WEEK (Week 9)

**Dates:** May 11 – May 16, 2026
**Target:** 79 kg → 77 kg (weigh-in morning of May 16)
**Strategy:** Water and sodium manipulation + glycogen depletion + minimal food intake

> **WARNING:** This protocol should be supervised. Monitor for signs of excessive dehydration: dizziness, dark urine, cramping, confusion. Have ORS (oral rehydration salts) on standby.

---

## Fight Week Daily Protocol

### Sunday (May 11) — Start Water Cut

| Timing | Food / Fluid | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **Morning** | 500 ml water with lemon | Sip, don't chug |
| **Breakfast** | 150 g chicken breast, 100 g cucumber, 50 g spinach | No carbs, no sodium |
| **Lunch** | 150 g white fish, large green salad (no dressing) | Low residue |
| **Water throughout day** | 2 L total (drastically reduced from 8 L) | Body still excreting at high rate |
| **Dinner** | 150 g chicken breast, 100 g asparagus | Asparagus is a natural diuretic |
| **Pre-bed** | 200 ml water | Small sips |
| **Sodium** | ZERO added sodium | Remove all salt |
| **Day total** | ~85 P / ~10 C / ~8 F / ~450 kcal | |

### Monday (May 12) — Deep Water Cut

| Timing | Food / Fluid | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **Morning** | 250 ml water | Sip |
| **Breakfast** | 3 egg whites, 50 g cucumber | Minimal |
| **Lunch** | 150 g chicken breast, 100 g celery | Low residue, diuretic foods |
| **Water throughout day** | 1 L total | Continue reducing |
| **Dinner** | 100 g chicken breast, 100 g asparagus | Light |
| **Sodium** | ZERO | Continue |
| **Day total** | ~70 P / ~6 C / ~5 F / ~350 kcal | |

### Tuesday (May 13) — Depletion

| Timing | Food / Fluid | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **Morning** | Small sips of water only | No more than 500 ml total for day |
| **Breakfast** | 3 egg whites | Dry food only |
| **Lunch** | 100 g chicken breast, small handful spinach | |
| **Dinner** | 100 g chicken breast | Keep stomach empty |
| **Water** | Max 500 ml total, sipped | |
| **Day total** | ~55 P / ~3 C / ~4 F / ~268 kcal | |

### Wednesday (May 14) — Final Dry-Out

| Timing | Food / Fluid | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **All day** | Minimal sips of water (250 ml max) | If weight is on target, stop cutting |
| **Food** | 100 g chicken breast if hungry, otherwise nothing | Keep gut empty |
| **Sweat session** | Light sauna or hot bath if needed (15 min max sessions, rest between) | Only if weight is not on target |
| **Monitor** | Check weight every 3–4 hours | |
| **Day total** | ~25 P / ~0 C / ~2 F / ~118 kcal (or less) | |

### Thursday (May 15) — Pre-Weigh-In

| Timing | Food / Fluid | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **Morning** | Zero water, zero food | Weigh yourself: if at 77 kg or under, proceed to weigh-in |
| **If over 77 kg** | Hot bath protocol: 10 min in, 10 min rest, repeat until weight is made | Maximum 3 sessions. Stop if dizzy. |
| **Weigh-in** | Make weight | |

---

## Post Weigh-In REHYDRATION & REFEED (Critical)

> Start rehydration IMMEDIATELY after weigh-in. This is as important as making weight.

### Immediate (0–30 min post weigh-in)

| Item | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| ORS solution (Pedialyte or equivalent) | 500 ml | Sip slowly over 30 min |
| Banana | 1 medium | Potassium replenishment |
| White bread with honey | 2 slices | Fast-digesting carbs |

### 1–2 hours post weigh-in

| Item | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| White rice (cooked) | 200 g | Easy carbs |
| Chicken breast | 150 g | Lean protein |
| Electrolyte drink | 500 ml | Continue sipping |
| Water | 500 ml | Sip, don't chug |

### 3–4 hours post weigh-in

| Item | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Pasta or rice | 250 g cooked | Glycogen reload |
| Chicken or fish | 200 g | Protein |
| Banana | 1 | More potassium |
| Water with electrolytes | 500 ml | |

### Evening before fight (5–8 hours post weigh-in)

| Item | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Familiar meal: rice + chicken + vegetables | Normal portion | Nothing exotic, nothing spicy |
| Fruit juice (orange or apple) | 250 ml | Sugar + hydration |
| Water | Sip freely | Stop chugging — you want to feel comfortable, not bloated |

### Fight Day Morning (May 16)

| Item | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Oatmeal (50 g) with honey and banana | 1 bowl | 3–4 hours before fight |
| Toast with peanut butter | 2 slices | Sustained energy |
| Water | 500 ml | Sip with breakfast |
| Coffee (optional) | 1 cup black | If part of normal routine |
| **30 min pre-fight** | 1 banana + small sip of sports drink | Quick energy |

---

## Supplement Protocol by Phase

| Supplement | Phase 1–3 | Phase 4 (Fight Week) | Post Weigh-In |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| Creatine monohydrate | 5 g/day | STOP (holds water) | Resume after fight |
| Fish oil | 2 g/day | Stop | Resume |
| Multivitamin | Daily | Daily | Daily |
| Magnesium | 400 mg before bed | 400 mg | 400 mg |
| Caffeine | As needed (black coffee) | Reduce to avoid dehydration | As needed |
| Electrolyte tabs | During training | Do not use (sodium) | Yes — immediately |
| BCAA | 10 g during training | Optional | Not needed |

---

## Grocery List Template (Weekly — Phases 1–3)

**Proteins:** 2.5 kg chicken breast, 500 g lean ground beef/turkey, 500 g white fish, 2 dozen eggs, 1 kg cottage cheese, 500 g Greek yogurt (0%), 2 tubs whey protein (monthly), 1 tub casein (monthly)

**Carbs:** 1 kg white rice, 1 kg sweet potatoes, 1 kg oats, wholegrain bread (1 loaf), rice cakes (1 pack), bananas (bunch of 6–8), mixed berries (300 g), apples (4–5)

**Fats:** 1 small bottle olive oil, 1 jar peanut butter (natural), 1 pack almonds (200 g), 2–3 avocados

**Vegetables:** Broccoli (500 g), spinach (300 g), asparagus (300 g), green beans (300 g), mixed salad bags, cucumbers, tomatoes, zucchini, peppers

**Other:** Honey, lemon, cinnamon, salt, pepper, garlic, electrolyte tablets, coffee

---

*This plan is designed for the specific weight cut from 89 kg to 77 kg over 9 weeks. All caloric targets are approximations — adjust based on daily weigh-ins and weekly progress. If weight loss exceeds 1.5 kg/week consistently, slightly increase carbs. If weight stalls for more than 5 days, reduce carbs by 25 g on rest days.*

# Exercise Guide

Click any exercise name in the training plan to jump here. Each exercise includes setup, execution, common mistakes, and how it transfers to MMA fighting.

---

## Back Squat

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, adductors, erectors  
**Equipment:** Barbell, squat rack

### Setup

Bar on upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar). Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out.

### Execution

Brace hard, spread the floor, hips and knees break together. Drive up through mid-foot.

### Common Mistakes

- Knees caving
- forward lean / chest drop
- cutting depth short
- losing brace at the bottom.

### MMA Application

Builds the lower-body force production that powers level changes, takedown defense (standing up under pressure), and knockout-level punching from the legs up.

---

## Front Squat

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, upper back, core  
**Equipment:** Barbell, squat rack

### Setup

Bar in front rack (clean grip or cross-arm). Elbows high, chest tall. Feet shoulder-width.

### Execution

Elbows up the whole way. Sit straight down between your hips. Drive up keeping torso vertical.

### Common Mistakes

- Elbows dropping (bar rolls forward)
- wrist inflexibility forcing cross-arm when clean grip is better
- shallow depth.

### MMA Application

Upright torso pattern mimics posture needed in the clinch and during double-leg finishes. Heavy anterior core demand transfers to resisting being bent over in grappling.

---

## Deadlift

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Posterior chain (glutes, hamstrings, erectors), lats, grip  
**Equipment:** Barbell

### Setup

Feet hip-width under bar, shins near bar. Hinge at hips, grip just outside knees. Neutral spine, lats engaged.

### Execution

Push the floor away, lock lats, bar drags up the legs. Stand tall — squeeze glutes at lockout.

### Common Mistakes

- Rounding the lower back
- jerking the bar off the floor
- hips shooting up first
- hyperextending at lockout.

### MMA Application

Total-body posterior chain strength for explosive hip extension — the engine behind throws, slams, sprawls, and heavy rear-hand strikes. Grip endurance under load.

---

## Romanian Deadlift (RDL)

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Hamstrings, glutes, erectors  
**Equipment:** Barbell

### Setup

Stand with bar at hip height (from rack or after a deadlift). Feet hip-width, slight knee bend, shoulders packed.

### Execution

Push hips straight back like closing a car door. Bar stays on the thighs/shins. Feel a deep hamstring stretch, then drive hips forward.

### Common Mistakes

- Bending the knees too much (turns into a squat)
- rounding the back
- not hinging far enough.

### MMA Application

Eccentric hamstring strength reduces knee-injury risk from sprawling and kicking. Hip-hinge patterning powers underhooks-to-trips and mat returns.

---

## Bench Press

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Pecs, anterior delts, triceps  
**Equipment:** Barbell, bench, rack

### Setup

Lie on bench, eyes under bar. Retract and depress shoulder blades. Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Feet flat on floor.

### Execution

Unrack, control bar to lower chest, drive through feet, press bar in a slight arc back over shoulders.

### Common Mistakes

- Flaring elbows to 90 degrees (shoulder stress)
- bouncing bar off chest
- losing scapular retraction
- lifting hips.

### MMA Application

Horizontal pressing power for frames, stiff-arms, and bench pressing an opponent off you from bottom position. Tricep strength aids in posting and creating distance.

---

## Overhead Press

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Deltoids, triceps, upper traps, core  
**Equipment:** Barbell, squat rack

### Setup

Bar in front rack at collarbone. Feet hip-width, glutes and abs braced.

### Execution

Press bar straight up — move your head out of the way, then back through once bar passes. Lock out directly over mid-foot.

### Common Mistakes

- Excessive back arch (turns into incline press)
- pressing the bar forward instead of straight up
- not bracing core.

### MMA Application

Overhead strength for posting on the cage, hand fighting from collar ties, and resisting being driven down in the clinch. Core stability under overhead load mirrors scramble demands.

---

## Barbell Row

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, rhomboids, rear delts, biceps, erectors  
**Equipment:** Barbell

### Setup

Hinge to roughly 45-degree torso. Overhand or underhand grip just outside knees. Slight knee bend.

### Execution

Pull bar to lower sternum, squeeze shoulder blades at top, control the negative. Torso stays still.

### Common Mistakes

- Excessive torso movement (rowing with momentum)
- pulling to the belly
- standing too upright.

### MMA Application

Pulling strength for clinch work, underhook control, arm drags, and guillotine finishes. Maintains upper-back posture to resist being broken down.

---

## Hang Clean

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, traps, upper back, forearms  
**Equipment:** Barbell

### Setup

Start standing with bar at hip height, overhand grip. Hinge slightly to bring bar to just above the knee (hang position).

### Execution

Explode hips, shrug, pull elbows high, catch in front rack with a quarter squat. Stand up.

### Common Mistakes

- Arm-pulling instead of hip-driving
- not getting elbows through fast enough
- catching on wrists instead of delts.

### MMA Application

Triple extension (ankle-knee-hip) is the same motor pattern as shooting a double-leg or throwing an uppercut. Teaches rate of force development — how fast you can express strength.

---

## Push Press

**Category:** Compound Barbell Lifts  
**Primary Muscles:** Deltoids, triceps, quads, glutes, core  
**Equipment:** Barbell, squat rack

### Setup

Bar in front rack at collarbone. Feet hip-width.

### Execution

Quick dip (4-6 inches) keeping torso vertical, then drive aggressively through legs and press bar overhead in one motion. Lock out.

### Common Mistakes

- Dipping forward (weight on toes)
- pressing before legs finish driving
- slow dip killing the stretch-shortening cycle.

### MMA Application

Teaches legs-to-arms force transfer — the same kinetic chain used in every punch and in off-balancing an opponent from the clinch. Develops overhead lockout power for cage work.

---

## RFESS (Bulgarian Split Squat)

**Category:** Single-Leg / Unilateral  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, adductors  
**Equipment:** Dumbbells or barbell, bench

### Setup

Rear foot laces-down on bench behind you. Front foot far enough forward that knee stays over ankle at bottom.

### Execution

Lower straight down until rear knee nearly touches floor. Drive through front heel. Keep torso upright.

### Common Mistakes

- Front foot too close to bench (knee shoots forward)
- torso leaning excessively
- rear foot bearing too much load.

### MMA Application

Single-leg strength and balance for kicking, sprawling off one leg, and maintaining base when an opponent grabs a leg. Reduces bilateral strength imbalances that cause injury.

---

## Single-Leg RDL

**Category:** Single-Leg / Unilateral  
**Primary Muscles:** Hamstrings, glutes, core (anti-rotation)  
**Equipment:** Dumbbell or kettlebell

### Setup

Stand on one leg, soft knee. Hold weight in opposite hand.

### Execution

Hinge at the hip, free leg extends behind you as a counterbalance. Back stays flat. Squeeze glute to stand.

### Common Mistakes

- Rounding the back
- opening the hip (rotating)
- not hinging deep enough
- rushing the movement.

### MMA Application

Posterior chain strength on one leg directly transfers to kicking (standing leg stability), single-leg takedown defense, and landing after throwing knees. Anti-rotation demand mirrors the rotational chaos of grappling.

---

## Walking Lunges

**Category:** Single-Leg / Unilateral  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, hip flexors  
**Equipment:** Dumbbells, barbell, or bodyweight

### Setup

Stand tall, weights at sides or on back.

### Execution

Take a long step forward, lower rear knee toward floor, drive through front heel to step into the next rep. Stay tall.

### Common Mistakes

- Short steps (knee over toe)
- torso collapsing forward
- pushing off rear foot instead of pulling with front leg.

### MMA Application

Dynamic single-leg strength with a locomotion component — transfers to shooting entries, level changes, and maintaining balance while moving forward in the pocket.

---

## Step-Ups

**Category:** Single-Leg / Unilateral  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes  
**Equipment:** Box (16-20 inches), dumbbells

### Setup

Place one foot fully on the box. Hold dumbbells at sides.

### Execution

Drive through the top foot to stand on the box. Do NOT push off the bottom foot. Control the descent.

### Common Mistakes

- Using the bottom foot to push off (cheating)
- box too low to challenge the movement
- leaning forward.

### MMA Application

Concentric single-leg power for driving off the mat (standing up from bottom), penetration steps for takedowns, and explosive level changes.

---

## Pistol Squat Progression

**Category:** Single-Leg / Unilateral  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, hip flexors, ankles  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight (band or box for assistance)

### Setup

Stand on one leg. Extend the other leg in front of you. Arms forward for counterbalance.

### Execution

Sit back and down on one leg, keeping heel flat. Go as low as mobility allows, then drive up. Use a band or box to assisted versions as needed.

### Common Mistakes

- Knee collapsing inward
- heel lifting
- rounding the lower back at the bottom
- using momentum to stand.

### MMA Application

Ultimate expression of single-leg strength and mobility. Translates to recovering balance after being swept or pushed, deep single-leg positions in scrambles, and demonstrating the mobility fighters need at the hips and ankles.

---

## Box Jumps

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, calves  
**Equipment:** Plyo box (20-30 inches)

### Setup

Stand facing box, feet shoulder-width, arms back.

### Execution

Swing arms, extend hips-knees-ankles, land softly on box with full foot contact. Stand tall, step down.

### Common Mistakes

- Rebounding off the floor (use a reset between reps)
- landing in a deep squat on the box (means the box is too high)
- jumping down instead of stepping.

### MMA Application

Trains rate of force development and reactive power for explosive takedown entries, sprawls, and closing distance to land strikes.

---

## Broad Jumps

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Glutes, quads, hamstrings, calves  
**Equipment:** Open floor space

### Setup

Feet shoulder-width, arms loaded behind you.

### Execution

Swing arms forward, drive hips and jump forward for max distance. Absorb landing with soft knees, stick it.

### Common Mistakes

- Not using the arms
- landing stiff-legged
- not committing to full hip extension.

### MMA Application

Horizontal explosive power — the exact vector used in shooting for takedowns and closing distance for punches. Teaches aggressive hip extension into a target.

---

## Med Ball Overhead Slam

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, core, shoulders, triceps  
**Equipment:** Slam ball (15-30 lbs)

### Setup

Stand tall, ball overhead with arms extended.

### Execution

Brace core, slam ball into the ground as hard as possible using your whole body. Hinge and follow through. Catch the bounce or pick it up.

### Common Mistakes

- Using only arms (not engaging core/hips)
- not following through
- too light a ball.

### MMA Application

Full-body power expression similar to throwing hammerfists from top position or slamming an opponent in a takedown finish. Teaches aggressive downward force production.

---

## Med Ball Rotational Slam

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Obliques, core, hips, shoulders  
**Equipment:** Slam ball (10-20 lbs), wall

### Setup

Stand perpendicular to wall, ball at hip opposite the wall. Feet slightly wider than shoulder-width.

### Execution

Rotate hips first, then trunk, then arms — slam ball into wall. Catch and reset. Load from the back hip.

### Common Mistakes

- Arm-throwing instead of hip-rotating
- feet staying flat (rotate on the ball of the back foot)
- standing too close to the wall.

### MMA Application

Directly trains the rotational power sequence of hooks, overhands, and round kicks. Hip-first rotation is the key to fight-ending power in strikes.

---

## Med Ball Chest Pass

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Pecs, triceps, anterior delts, core  
**Equipment:** Med ball (10-20 lbs), wall or partner

### Setup

Stand facing wall, ball at chest, elbows in.

### Execution

Drive ball off chest explosively into wall, extending arms fully. Catch and immediately redirect. Stay on the balls of your feet.

### Common Mistakes

- Stepping forward (losing base)
- lobbing instead of driving
- slow catch-and-redirect.

### MMA Application

Trains horizontal pushing speed for frames, posting, and bench-pressing an opponent off you. Reactive catch-and-redirect mirrors the speed demands of hand fighting.

---

## KB Swings

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Glutes, hamstrings, core, lats  
**Equipment:** Kettlebell

### Setup

Feet slightly wider than shoulder-width. KB on floor a foot in front of you. Hike the bell back between legs.

### Execution

Snap hips forward — the arms just go along for the ride. Bell should float to chest/eye height (Russian) or overhead (American). Squeeze glutes hard at the top.

### Common Mistakes

- Squatting the swing (should be a hinge)
- using the arms to lift
- hyperextending the back at the top.

### MMA Application

Powerful hip snap transfers directly to striking power, sprawl speed, and bridging/shrimping in grappling. High-rep swings also build posterior chain endurance for later rounds.

---

## KB Snatch

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, traps, core  
**Equipment:** Kettlebell

### Setup

Similar to swing start. One hand on the bell.

### Execution

Hike back, snap hips, as the bell rises guide it around your wrist and punch through to lockout overhead. Control the descent by redirecting the bell back between your legs.

### Common Mistakes

- Bell banging the wrist (path is wrong)
- pressing out the top instead of punching through
- not using the hips.

### MMA Application

Combines explosive hip extension with overhead stability and grip — mirrors the full-body power expression of throwing an overhand right or finishing a high-crotch lift. Excellent conditioning effect.

## Battle Rope Slams

**Category:** Power / Explosive  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, core, lats, grip  
**Equipment:** Battle ropes (1.5-2 inch, 40-50 ft)

### Setup

Stand in athletic stance, slight hinge, one rope end in each hand. Anchor point ahead.

### Execution

Alternate arms (waves) or slam both together. Generate force from the hips, not just the shoulders. Keep a rhythm — don't let the ropes go slack.

### Common Mistakes

- Standing too upright (no hip involvement)
- death-gripping (forearms burn out instantly)
- standing too close to the anchor (no tension).

### MMA Application

Upper-body power-endurance under fatigue — simulates the repeated explosive arm actions of punching in later rounds. Bilateral slams train ground-and-pound power; alternating waves build the shoulder endurance needed for long clinch exchanges.

---

## Weighted Pull-Ups

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, biceps, rear delts, grip  
**Equipment:** Pull-up bar, dip belt or dumbbell between feet

### Setup

Hang from bar, overhand grip slightly wider than shoulders. Add load via belt or dumbbell.

### Execution

Initiate with lats (think elbows to hips), pull chin over bar, control the descent to full extension.

### Common Mistakes

- Kipping / swinging
- not reaching full extension at the bottom
- chin not clearing the bar.

### MMA Application

Pulling strength is essential for clinch control, guillotines, front headlocks, arm drags, and dragging an opponent down from the cage. Grip under load builds lasting clinch endurance.

---

## Dumbbell Row

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, rhomboids, rear delts, biceps  
**Equipment:** Dumbbell, bench

### Setup

One knee and hand on bench, opposite foot on floor. Flat back, dumbbell hanging at arm's length.

### Execution

Pull dumbbell to hip, squeezing shoulder blade back and down. Lower under control.

### Common Mistakes

- Rotating the torso to heave the weight
- rowing to the shoulder instead of the hip
- rushing reps.

### MMA Application

Unilateral pulling strength for single-collar ties, underhook battles, and controlling an arm in guard. Addresses left-right imbalances common in fighters with a dominant stance.

---

## Landmine Press

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Chest, shoulders, triceps, core (anti-rotation)  
**Equipment:** Barbell, landmine attachment (or corner)

### Setup

Stand in split stance, hold the end of the barbell at shoulder height with one hand.

### Execution

Press the bar up and forward at a slight angle. Squeeze the core to prevent rotation. Control the return.

### Common Mistakes

- Flaring the elbow
- rotating the torso
- not bracing the core
- pressing straight up instead of along the bar's arc.

### MMA Application

The angled pressing path closely mimics a jab and cross. Single-arm execution demands anti-rotation — the same core stability needed when posting off an opponent or framing from bottom.

---

## Incline DB Press

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Upper pecs, anterior delts, triceps  
**Equipment:** Dumbbells, adjustable bench (30-45 degrees)

### Setup

Sit on incline bench, dumbbells at shoulders, feet flat on floor, back pressed into pad.

### Execution

Press dumbbells up and slightly inward, without clanking them at the top. Lower to shoulders with control.

### Common Mistakes

- Over-arching the back (reducing the incline angle)
- flaring elbows
- bouncing at the bottom.

### MMA Application

Upper chest and shoulder pressing strength for frames from bottom, posting on the cage, and pushing an opponent away. Dumbbell path allows shoulder-friendly range of motion for fighters with existing shoulder wear.

---

## Push-Up — Standard

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Pecs, triceps, anterior delts, core  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Hands shoulder-width, body in a straight line from head to heels.

### Execution

Lower chest to the floor, elbows at 45 degrees, press back up. Lock out at top.

### Common Mistakes

- Sagging hips
- flaring elbows to 90 degrees
- partial range of motion.

### MMA Application

Foundational pressing endurance for maintaining frames, escaping bottom positions, and posting. High-rep capacity means pressing strength that lasts into championship rounds.

---

## Push-Up — Diamond

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Triceps, inner chest, core  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Hands together under chest forming a diamond shape with index fingers and thumbs.

### Execution

Keep elbows tight to the body as you lower. Touch chest to hands, press up.

### Common Mistakes

- Flaring elbows
- letting hips sag
- reducing range of motion.

### MMA Application

Tricep-dominant pressing for short-range pushing — creating frames inside the guard and posting off an opponent's head in scrambles.

---

## Push-Up — Archer

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Chest, triceps, core  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Wide hand placement. One arm bends while the other stays extended to the side.

### Execution

Lower toward one hand, keeping the other arm straight as a guide. Push back up. Alternate sides.

### Common Mistakes

- Rotating the torso
- bending the straight arm
- shortening the range.

### MMA Application

Asymmetric pressing strength for single-arm frames and posts — common in scrambles where only one arm is available for base. Bridges the gap toward one-arm push-up strength.

---

## Push-Up — Clap

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Pecs, triceps, fast-twitch fibers  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Standard push-up position.

### Execution

Explode off the floor, clap, land with soft elbows, immediately descend into the next rep.

### Common Mistakes

- Landing with locked elbows (injury risk)
- losing body alignment
- not achieving full flight.

### MMA Application

Upper-body plyometric power for explosive frames and the initial burst of a punch. Trains the stretch-shortening cycle in horizontal pressing — speed kills in striking.

---

## Dips

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Triceps, lower chest, anterior delts  
**Equipment:** Parallel bars or rings

### Setup

Grip the bars, arms locked, slight forward lean.

### Execution

Lower until upper arms are parallel to floor, press back up to full lockout. Keep elbows tracking over wrists.

### Common Mistakes

- Going too deep (shoulder stress)
- excessive forward lean
- partial range of motion.

### MMA Application

Pressing strength from a depressed shoulder position — directly transfers to posting on the mat, pressing up from bottom, and generating pushing force when pinned against the cage.

---

## Face Pulls

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Rear delts, external rotators, mid traps  
**Equipment:** Cable machine or band

### Setup

Rope attachment at face height. Step back to create tension.

### Execution

Pull rope to face, splitting hands apart and externally rotating at the end. Squeeze for a beat. Slow return.

### Common Mistakes

- Pulling too low (becomes a row)
- using body momentum
- not externally rotating at the end position.

### MMA Application

Shoulder health and postural balance. Fighters do enormous volumes of pressing and internal rotation (punching, pushing); face pulls counterbalance this to keep shoulders healthy and maintain posture against collar ties.

---

## Band Pull-Aparts

**Category:** Upper Body Accessories  
**Primary Muscles:** Rear delts, rhomboids, mid traps  
**Equipment:** Resistance band

### Setup

Hold band at arm's length in front of chest, hands shoulder-width, palms down.

### Execution

Pull band apart by squeezing shoulder blades together until band touches chest. Slow return.

### Common Mistakes

- Shrugging shoulders
- bending elbows
- using momentum.

### MMA Application

Scapular health and posture maintenance. Keeps the upper back strong and shoulders balanced against the high volume of punching and reaching in training. Ideal daily-dose prehab movement.

---

## Pallof Press

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Obliques, transverse abdominis, hip stabilizers  
**Equipment:** Cable machine or band

### Setup

Stand perpendicular to cable, handle at chest. Feet shoulder-width, slight athletic bend.

### Execution

Press handle straight out from chest and hold. Resist the rotation. Return to chest. Keep hips square.

### Common Mistakes

- Letting the cable rotate you
- locking knees
- pressing at an angle instead of straight out.

### MMA Application

Anti-rotation core stability — the ability to resist being twisted or turned in the clinch, during scrambles, and when absorbing body shots. Directly trains the core's primary fight function: resisting unwanted movement.

---

## Ab Wheel Rollout

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Rectus abdominis, obliques, lats, hip flexors  
**Equipment:** Ab wheel

### Setup

Kneel on a pad, grip the wheel, arms extended.

### Execution

Roll forward slowly, keeping hips extended (don't sit back). Go as far as you can control, then pull back using your abs.

### Common Mistakes

- Sagging the lower back
- bending at the hips instead of extending
- going farther than you can control.

### MMA Application

Anti-extension core strength for maintaining posture in the clinch, resisting back bends in wrestling, and stabilizing the spine during strikes. The eccentric challenge mirrors being pulled forward by an opponent.

---

## Hanging Leg Raises

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Hip flexors, lower abs, grip  
**Equipment:** Pull-up bar

### Setup

Hang from bar with full grip, shoulders packed.

### Execution

Raise legs to parallel (or toes to bar for advanced). Control the descent — no swinging. Initiate from the core, not momentum.

### Common Mistakes

- Swinging
- using momentum
- only lifting knees (if full leg raises are the target)
- losing grip.

### MMA Application

Hip flexor strength for guard retention, high kicks, and knee strikes. Grip endurance while performing a core movement mirrors the simultaneous demands of hanging on an opponent while working your core in the clinch.

---

## Copenhagen Plank

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Adductors, obliques, core  
**Equipment:** Bench

### Setup

Side-lying, top foot on bench, bottom foot hanging. Support on forearm.

### Execution

Lift hips and bottom leg so the body forms a straight line. Hold. The top leg's adductor does the heavy lifting.

### Common Mistakes

- Rotating hips forward or back
- sagging at the hip
- holding breath.

### MMA Application

Adductor and lateral core strength critical for closed guard, triangle setups, body lock squeezes, and injury prevention during lateral movement and kicking. Groin injuries are common in MMA — this is direct armor.

---

## Dead Bug

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Deep core stabilizers, hip flexors  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight (mat)

### Setup

Lie on back, arms reaching to ceiling, knees bent at 90 degrees over hips. Press low back into floor.

### Execution

Extend opposite arm and leg toward the floor while keeping the low back glued to the mat. Return and switch sides.

### Common Mistakes

- Low back arching off the floor
- moving too fast
- not exhaling fully on extension.

### MMA Application

Teaches the core to stabilize the spine while the limbs move independently — exactly what happens when striking, grappling, and transitioning between positions. Foundation for all other core work.

---

## Bird Dog

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Erectors, glutes, deep core stabilizers  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight (mat)

### Setup

Hands-and-knees position, wrists under shoulders, knees under hips.

### Execution

Extend opposite arm and leg until parallel with the floor. Pause. Return. Keep hips level — no rotation.

### Common Mistakes

- Rotating the hips
- arching the back
- rushing through reps
- lifting the limbs too high.

### MMA Application

Anti-rotation and anti-extension in a quadruped position — directly mimics the demands of maintaining base in turtle position and during scrambles on the mat. Excellent warm-up and prehab movement.

---

## Farmer's Carry

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Traps, grip, core (anti-lateral flexion), whole body  
**Equipment:** Heavy dumbbells or farmer's walk handles

### Setup

Stand tall with heavy weights at your sides. Shoulders packed, chest up.

### Execution

Walk with short, controlled steps. Stay tall — don't lean. Squeeze the handles hard. Breathe.

### Common Mistakes

- Leaning to one side
- taking long strides
- shrugging the shoulders
- letting posture break down.

### MMA Application

Total-body stability, grip endurance, and postural strength under load. Directly transfers to clinch endurance, maintaining posture while gripping an opponent, and the general full-body tension required in grappling.

---

## Suitcase Carry

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Obliques, core (anti-lateral flexion), grip  
**Equipment:** Heavy dumbbell or kettlebell

### Setup

Hold a heavy weight in one hand at your side. Stand tall, don't lean toward or away from the load.

### Execution

Walk with controlled steps, resisting the urge to lean toward the loaded side. Stay perfectly upright.

### Common Mistakes

- Leaning toward the weight
- compensating with the opposite hip
- shrugging the loaded shoulder.

### MMA Application

Unilateral anti-lateral flexion — the ability to resist being pulled to one side. Transfers to single-collar tie defense, resisting an arm drag, and maintaining posture when an opponent pulls one side of your body.

---

## Turkish Get-Up

**Category:** Core / Anti-Movement  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, core, hips, stabilizers (full body)  
**Equipment:** Kettlebell

### Setup

Lie on back, press kettlebell up with one arm. Opposite arm and leg angled out for base.

### Execution

Roll to elbow, post on hand, bridge hips, sweep leg under to kneeling, stand up. Reverse the sequence. Eyes on the bell at all times.

### Common Mistakes

- Rushing the movement
- losing visual contact with the bell
- skipping the hip bridge
- pressing the bell instead of keeping the arm vertical.

### MMA Application

The most MMA-specific core exercise. Every phase mimics a grappling transition: bridging from bottom, posting up to an elbow, creating a frame, building base, and standing from the mat — all while stabilizing load overhead (simulating controlling an opponent's limb).

---

## Band External Rotations

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Infraspinatus, teres minor (rotator cuff)  
**Equipment:** Light resistance band

### Setup

Stand with elbow pinned to side at 90 degrees, band attached at elbow height.

### Execution

Rotate forearm outward against band, keeping elbow glued to your ribs. Slow and controlled both directions.

### Common Mistakes

- Elbow drifting away from the body
- using too heavy a band
- moving too fast.

### MMA Application

Rotator cuff health is non-negotiable for fighters. The shoulder absorbs enormous stress from punching, posting, and being manipulated in grappling. This keeps the external rotators strong enough to balance the dominant internal rotators.

## 90/90 Hip Switches

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Hip internal and external rotators, glutes  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight (mat)

### Setup

Sit on floor with one leg in front (shin perpendicular, externally rotated) and one behind (shin perpendicular, internally rotated). Both knees at 90 degrees.

### Execution

Rotate both legs to the opposite position in one smooth motion. Keep chest tall. Pause at each end position.

### Common Mistakes

- Leaning back
- using hands excessively
- rushing through transitions
- knees not staying at 90 degrees.

### MMA Application

Hip mobility in both internal and external rotation — essential for guard play, sweeps, hip escapes, and kicking. Fighters with restricted hips compensate with their spine, leading to back injuries.

---

## Hip CARs (Controlled Articular Rotations)

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Hip joint (all surrounding musculature)  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

On hands and knees (or standing). Lift one knee to the side.

### Execution

Make the largest possible circle with your knee — up, out, back, and around — using only the hip joint. Move slowly and with maximum tension. Reverse direction.

### Common Mistakes

- Using momentum
- moving the spine to fake hip range
- rushing
- not engaging max tension.

### MMA Application

Maintaining full usable hip range of motion. Fighters lose hip mobility over time from tight guards, repeated impact, and heavy squatting. CARs preserve the range needed for high kicks, rubber guard, and deep half guard.

---

## Thoracic Spine Rotations (Open Books)

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Thoracic spine rotators, obliques  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight (mat)

### Setup

Lie on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90 degrees. Arms extended in front, palms together.

### Execution

Rotate the top arm up and over, opening the chest to the ceiling. Follow the hand with your eyes. Let the thoracic spine rotate — keep knees glued together.

### Common Mistakes

- Rotating from the lumbar spine (knees separate)
- not fully opening
- rushing.

### MMA Application

Thoracic rotation is the engine of punching power and shot defense. Fighters who lose T-spine rotation compensate with the lower back and shoulders, leading to chronic pain. This preserves the rotation needed for hooks, elbows, and looking over the shoulder to defend takedowns.

---

## Goblet Squat

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, core  
**Equipment:** Kettlebell or dumbbell

### Setup

Hold weight at chest height, elbows inside knees. Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out.

### Execution

Sit between your hips, elbows push knees out at the bottom. Chest tall, drive through whole foot.

### Common Mistakes

- Leaning forward
- heels rising
- not hitting depth.

### MMA Application

Warm-up and movement prep for squatting patterns. Teaches an upright squat position that transfers to posture in the clinch and double-leg finishes.

---

## Glute Bridge

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Glutes, hamstrings  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight or barbell (mat)

### Setup

Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat on floor hip-width apart. Arms at sides.

### Execution

Drive hips to ceiling by squeezing glutes. Hold for a beat at the top. Lower with control.

### Common Mistakes

- Hyperextending the lower back
- driving through toes instead of heels
- not achieving full hip extension.

### MMA Application

Hip extension activation for warm-ups. Directly mimics the bridge escape from bottom mount — one of the most fundamental grappling movements.

---

## Band Dislocates

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, rotator cuff, pecs  
**Equipment:** Resistance band or PVC pipe

### Setup

Hold band/pipe with wide overhand grip in front of hips.

### Execution

Keeping arms straight, raise the band overhead and behind your back in a slow arc. Reverse. Widen grip if needed.

### Common Mistakes

- Bending elbows
- going too fast
- using too narrow a grip before mobility allows.

### MMA Application

Full shoulder range-of-motion warm-up. Prepares the shoulder capsule for pressing and overhead work while counteracting the tight anterior shoulders fighters develop from punching and clinch work.

---

## Scap Push-Ups

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Serratus anterior, lower traps  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Standard push-up position with arms locked.

### Execution

Without bending the elbows, protract shoulder blades (push floor away) then retract (let chest sink). Small controlled movement.

### Common Mistakes

- Bending the elbows (turning it into a push-up)
- moving too fast
- excessive range.

### MMA Application

Serratus anterior activation for scapular stability. Protects the shoulder during pressing and posting — especially important for fighters who spend time on their hands in grappling.

---

## PVC Overhead Squat

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, thoracic spine, hips, ankles  
**Equipment:** PVC pipe or broomstick

### Setup

Hold PVC overhead with wide snatch-width grip. Feet shoulder-width.

### Execution

Squat to full depth while keeping the PVC directly overhead and arms locked. Chest tall, heels down.

### Common Mistakes

- Bar drifting forward
- heels lifting
- losing thoracic extension
- excessive forward lean.

### MMA Application

Full-body mobility diagnostic and warm-up. Reveals restrictions in ankles, hips, thoracic spine, and shoulders simultaneously. Builds the overhead stability needed for sprawling with arms extended.

---

## KB Halo

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, core, upper back  
**Equipment:** Kettlebell

### Setup

Stand tall, hold KB by the horns upside-down at chest height.

### Execution

Circle the KB around your head in a smooth arc, keeping it close. Alternate directions each rep. Brace the core.

### Common Mistakes

- Leaning the torso
- moving the head instead of the KB
- going too fast.

### MMA Application

Shoulder warm-up with rotational demand. Prepares shoulders for pressing while building the circular shoulder mobility needed for clinch pummeling and underhook battles.

---

## Banded Lateral Walk

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Glute medius, hip abductors  
**Equipment:** Mini band

### Setup

Place band around ankles or just above knees. Quarter-squat athletic stance.

### Execution

Take controlled lateral steps, maintaining tension on the band. Don't let knees cave. Stay low.

### Common Mistakes

- Standing too upright
- letting the trailing leg snap back
- knees collapsing inward.

### MMA Application

Glute medius activation for lateral movement stability. Strengthens the hip abductors that stabilize the stance during kicks, lateral cage cutting, and single-leg takedown defense.

---

## World's Greatest Stretch

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Hip flexors, thoracic spine, hamstrings, groin  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Step into a deep lunge position, place both hands on the floor inside the front foot.

### Execution

Rotate the inside arm up toward the ceiling, opening the chest. Hold 2-3 seconds. Return hand to floor. Straighten front leg for hamstring stretch. Repeat other side.

### Common Mistakes

- Rushing through positions
- not rotating fully
- back knee slamming the floor.

### MMA Application

The single most efficient dynamic warm-up movement. Addresses hip flexor tightness, thoracic rotation, hamstring length, and groin mobility in one sequence — all areas that take a beating during MMA training.

---

## Wall Ankle Stretch

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Soleus, Achilles, anterior tibialis  
**Equipment:** Wall

### Setup

Place one foot a few inches from a wall, other foot behind for support. Front knee drives forward toward the wall.

### Execution

Push the knee over the toes toward the wall without the heel lifting. Move the foot farther back to increase difficulty.

### Common Mistakes

- Heel lifting off the ground
- knee collapsing inward
- not progressing distance.

### MMA Application

Ankle dorsiflexion is required for deep squat positions, shooting levels changes, and absorbing force when moving laterally. Restricted ankles cause compensatory knee and hip issues — common in fighters who skip mobility work.

## Prone Y/T/W Raises

**Category:** Prehab / Mobility  
**Primary Muscles:** Lower traps, mid traps, rotator cuff, rear delts  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight or light dumbbells (2-5 lbs)

### Setup

Lie face-down on floor or incline bench. Arms hanging.

### Execution

Y: raise arms overhead in a Y-shape, thumbs up. T: raise arms to sides in a T. W: pull elbows down and back into a W, squeezing shoulder blades. Hold each top position for 2 seconds.

### Common Mistakes

- Using too much weight
- shrugging the traps instead of activating lower/mid traps
- moving too fast.

### MMA Application

Lower and mid trap activation combats the rounded-shoulder posture caused by heavy bag work, grappling, and desk sitting. Healthy scapular mechanics protect the shoulder during armbars, kimuras, and posted landings.

---

## Plate Pinch Holds

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** Finger flexors, thumb, forearm  
**Equipment:** Weight plates (two smooth-side-out)

### Setup

Pinch two plates together (smooth sides out) between fingers and thumb. Stand tall.

### Execution

Squeeze the plates as hard as possible. Hold for time. Keep shoulders level.

### Common Mistakes

- Using bumper plates (too easy to grip)
- letting the plates slide
- shrugging.

### MMA Application

Pinch grip strength for controlling wrists during grappling, maintaining grip on a sweaty opponent, and securing collar ties. The thumb-dominant grip pattern is specific to controlling limbs.

---

## Towel Pull-Ups

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, forearms, finger flexors  
**Equipment:** Pull-up bar, two gym towels

### Setup

Drape towels over the bar, grip one in each hand.

### Execution

Perform pull-ups while gripping the towels. Squeeze hard — if your grip fails, the set is over.

### Common Mistakes

- Wrapping towels around the hands (defeats the purpose)
- kipping
- cutting range of motion.

### MMA Application

Grip endurance on a thick, pliable surface mimics gripping a gi or controlling a sweaty wrist/ankle. The forearm and finger fatigue is directly transferable to late-round grappling grip.

---

## Fat Grip Rows

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, rhomboids, forearms  
**Equipment:** Barbell or dumbbells, Fat Gripz / thick bar

### Setup

Attach Fat Gripz to the bar or use a thick-handle bar. Set up for a standard row.

### Execution

Row as normal, but the thick handle forces a much harder grip. Reduce weight by 20-30% compared to normal rows.

### Common Mistakes

- Using the same weight as regular rows (grip fails before back)
- squeezing too tentatively.

### MMA Application

Thick-bar training builds the open-hand grip strength used in no-gi grappling, where you cannot close your fingers around a collar or sleeve. Stronger hands mean better wrist control and submission defense.

---

## Gi Pull-Ups

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** Lats, forearms, finger flexors  
**Equipment:** Pull-up bar, old gi or gi pants

### Setup

Drape a gi over the pull-up bar. Grip the lapels or fabric.

### Execution

Pull up while gripping the gi material. Vary grip positions — lapel, sleeve, collar. Hold at the top for extra credit.

### Common Mistakes

- Using thin fabric that tears
- wrapping the gi around hands instead of gripping
- partial range of motion.

### MMA Application

The most sport-specific grip exercise for gi-based grappling. Even in no-gi and MMA, the wrist-and-fabric-type grip endurance transfers to controlling rashguards and maintaining wrist control.

---

## Neck Harness (4-Way)

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** Sternocleidomastoid, upper traps, neck extensors, lateral flexors  
**Equipment:** Neck harness, weight plate or loading pin

### Setup

Attach weight to harness. Perform flexion (chin to chest), extension (look up), and lateral flexion (ear to shoulder) from a seated or standing hinged position.

### Execution

Controlled range of motion through the full movement. 2-second hold at end range. Start very light (5-10 lbs) and add load slowly over weeks.

### Common Mistakes

- Going too heavy too soon
- jerking the weight
- hyperextending at end range
- skipping warm-up sets.

### MMA Application

A strong neck directly reduces concussion risk by limiting rapid head acceleration from strikes. Neck strength also resists guillotines, can openers, and cranks. This is the most injury-preventive exercise a fighter can do.

---

## Band Neck Isometrics

**Category:** Grip / Neck  
**Primary Muscles:** All neck musculature  
**Equipment:** Resistance band

### Setup

Loop band around the head (forehead for extension resistance, back of head for flexion resistance, side for lateral). Anchor band at head height.

### Execution

Hold head in a neutral position against the band's pull. 10-20 second holds. Rotate through all four directions.

### Common Mistakes

- Band too heavy (neck compensates with torso movement)
- not training all four directions
- holding breath.

### MMA Application

Low-impact neck strengthening ideal for daily training. Builds isometric neck endurance — the type of strength needed to resist a head-position change in real time when being punched or caught in a front headlock. Safe enough to perform at every session.

---

## Assault Bike Intervals

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, hamstrings, shoulders, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** Assault / air bike

### Setup

Adjust seat height so there's a slight bend in the knee at the bottom. Grip the handles.

### Execution

Push and pull with arms, drive with legs. For intervals: all-out effort for the work period, easy spin for rest. Focus on RPM or calories as your target.

### Common Mistakes

- Sitting too high (hip rocking)
- arms and legs not coordinated
- not going truly all-out during work intervals.

### MMA Application

Whole-body energy system training with zero impact. The concurrent arm and leg work mimics the metabolic demand of fighting. Intervals (e.g., 10s on / 20s off) replicate the explosive-burst-then-recover pattern of MMA rounds.

---

## Rowing — 500m Repeats

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Legs, back, arms, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** Rowing ergometer (Concept2)

### Setup

Strap feet in, set damper to 4-6. Start with arms extended, shins vertical.

### Execution

Drive with legs first, then open the hips, then pull handle to sternum. Reverse the order on the recovery. Target consistent split times across repeats.

### Common Mistakes

- Pulling with the arms first (should be legs-hips-arms)
- setting damper too high
- inconsistent pacing.

### MMA Application

Full posterior chain conditioning with a pulling emphasis — balances the push-dominant demands of striking. 500m repeats at race pace build the lactic tolerance needed for scramble-heavy rounds and late takedown battles.

---

## Jump Rope

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Calves, shoulders, coordination, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** Speed rope

### Setup

Hold handles at hip height, elbows tight to body. Stand on balls of feet.

### Execution

Small bounces — just enough to clear the rope. Turn from the wrists, not the shoulders. Stay relaxed.

### Common Mistakes

- Jumping too high
- turning from the elbows
- tension in the shoulders
- double-bouncing.

### MMA Application

Footwork coordination, calf endurance, and rhythm — the foundation of striking movement. Builds the light, bouncy base that allows fighters to maintain distance, change angles, and stay on the balls of their feet for five rounds.

---

## Sled Push / Pull

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, core  
**Equipment:** Sled, turf or smooth surface

### Setup

Load the sled. For pushes: hands on high or low handles, lean in. For pulls: attach a strap or rope and face the sled.

### Execution

Push: drive with legs, keep arms extended, stay low. Pull: sit back and row hand-over-hand, or backpedal with the strap over your shoulders.

### Common Mistakes

- Standing too upright on pushes (losing drive angle)
- loading too heavy and losing speed
- resting between steps.

### MMA Application

Zero eccentric load means heavy conditioning without delayed-onset soreness — critical for fighters who need to train skill work the next day. Pushing mimics cage driving; pulling mimics dragging an opponent. Both build leg drive capacity.

---

## Sledgehammer Tire Strikes

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Core, shoulders, lats, grip, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** Sledgehammer (10-20 lbs), large tire

### Setup

Stand in a staggered stance facing the tire, hammer overhead with a slight chop angle.

### Execution

Slide the top hand up during the lift, slide it down to meet the bottom hand during the strike. Rotate hips into each swing. Alternate sides.

### Common Mistakes

- Gripping too tight (grip fatigue)
- all arms / no hip rotation
- not alternating lead side.

### MMA Application

Rotational power-endurance — the ability to throw powerful, hip-driven strikes repeatedly. The tool's weight and deceleration demand mirror the impact absorption of hitting pads or an opponent. Builds fight-specific grip endurance.

---

## Shadow Boxing

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, core, legs, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** None (gloves optional)

### Setup

Fight stance, hands up, chin tucked, weight on balls of feet.

### Execution

Throw combinations with intent. Move your feet between combos. Visualize an opponent — don't just wave your hands. Work defense between offense.

### Common Mistakes

- Dropping the hands
- flat-footed movement
- no defensive movement between combos
- punching with no purpose.

### MMA Application

The most specific conditioning modality for striking. Allows technical practice under fatigue. When used in training programs, shadow boxing rounds serve as active recovery while reinforcing technique and ring generalship.

---

## Bag Work

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Shoulders, core, legs, arms, cardiovascular system  
**Equipment:** Heavy bag (70-100 lbs), bag gloves or MMA gloves, hand wraps

### Setup

Wrap hands, glove up. Set up at punching range from the bag. Fight stance.

### Execution

Throw real combinations — jab-cross-hook, not single shots. Move your head and feet between combos. Hit through the bag, not at it.

### Common Mistakes

- Standing flat-footed and winging punches
- not moving between combos
- hitting with bad wrist alignment
- going 100% every round without variation.

### MMA Application

Full-contact striking conditioning that builds punch endurance, timing, and the ability to maintain technique under fatigue. Programmed in 3-5 minute rounds to match fight timing. The bag's feedback teaches fighters to commit to their shots.

---

## Sprawl-to-Burpee

**Category:** Conditioning Modalities  
**Primary Muscles:** Full body (hip flexors, quads, chest, shoulders, cardiovascular system)  
**Equipment:** Bodyweight

### Setup

Stand in fight stance.

### Execution

Drop into a sprawl (hips to floor, legs back, chest up), immediately pop back to feet, then jump (or skip the jump for speed). Reset stance and repeat.

### Common Mistakes

- Lazy sprawls (hips stay high — defeats the purpose)
- not returning to a fight stance
- pacing too slow for conditioning effect.

### MMA Application

The most MMA-specific bodyweight conditioning movement. Combines takedown defense (the sprawl) with explosive recovery to standing. High-rep sets build the anaerobic endurance to stuff takedowns in the championship rounds when most fights are decided.
